key_insight: A two-stage eye-tracking classifier could distinguish Parkinson’s disease from controls and other movement disorders with promising end-to-end diagnostic performance.
clinical_relevance: The study suggests eye-tracking may serve as a non-invasive biomarker to support earlier, more objective Parkinson’s diagnosis, but it needs multicenter confirmation before clinical use.
method: 90 participants total: 30 Parkinson’s disease, 30 other movement disorders, 30 healthy controls.
Two 10-minute sessions on a 250-Hz binocular eye-tracking system.
Elastic-net feature selection followed by logistic regression in a two-level cascade classifier, evaluated with 10-fold cross-validation.
evidence_signal: End-to-end Parkinson’s detection reached AUC 0.866 and 83.5% accuracy, with Level 1 AUC 0.818 and Level 2 AUC 0.670 in this proof-of-concept study.
watch_item: Single-center, small-sample validation with modest Level 2 performance means results may not generalize without larger multicenter testing.
paper_title: Automated Eye-Tracking for Parkinson's Disease Diagnosis: A Proof-of-Concept Cascade Classifier Study Establishing Clinical Validity
